text:
oted the authority of sages like PardSara, but quoted only a single
verse of Devala. On the other hand, he quotes eight verses from
each of Yavaneivara and the author's Samdsa-sarhhitd. This leads us
to suspect that by Utpala*s time the literature of ancient sages on
this subject might have been well nigh lost.
(u) He quotes two verses (under V. 11) of the Suryasiddhdnta,
which are not available in its modern version. This leads to the
conclusion that the version that was studied by was materially
different.
(iii) Utpala quotes Rdtamdn(j.avya on prosody and Mdnrfavya
separately. This name, however, has been mentioned by Al-Biruni
(I. 157) as that of the author of a Samhitd, which is quite possible,
since the commentator quotes him in the B.J.1. It is to be borne in
mind that the name Mdn^avya occurs in many Pur anas, the Rdmdyana
etc. It is quite possible that there were many sages of that name in
ancient times.
